text: De testamento is a treatise concerning the theological and biblical aspects of the covenant. Bullinger expounds on a number of themes here that are important to his theology, for example justification by faith, God’s accommodation to humanity, that the covenant is the theme or content of Scripture, that there is a covenant with Adam, that there is unity between the Old and New Testaments, that the seed of Abraham is Christ, that the covenant has to do with righteous living, that the Holy Spirit is the Spirit of the covenant, that the Law is the covenant, that the conditions of the covenant have to do with faith, and that the ceremonies of the covenant are related to the sacrament of the covenant. In De testamento Bullinger emerges as a biblical theologian urging the elect to live the whole way of life of the integrated man before the sovereign El Shaddai. An examination of Bullinger’s use of the Latin words for “covenant” (foedus, testamentum, and pactum) shows that to Bullinger they are interchangeable. Bullinger prefers not to use pactum in order to avoid being contaminated with the pactum theology of others. Bullinger’s use of the terms is more consistent than in the case of Zwingli, a fact which shows that Bullinger was not heavily influenced by the way in which Zwingli used these terms.
explanation: The passage reads like a concise, well-structured scholarly summary with specific historical details, which could be produced by a human expert or by an advanced AI. Given the balanced tone and coherent argument, it is moderately likely to be AI-generated.
